sub_topics.title: Importance of Agriculture
competences: Demonstrate understanding of the importance of Agriculture in Zambia

teacher_steps: Classifying Agriculture as an applied science or as a technology
Investigating the value of agriculture (food provision, food security, provision of raw materials, employment, income generation and foreign exchange...)
Collecting data, discussing and writing down the importance of agriculture in Zambia
Evaluating the impact of underdevelopment of agriculture in Zambia
Developing new ideas and innovations on how to improve agriculture in Zambia
learner_steps: Participate in classifying agriculture as science or technology
Research and collect data on agricultural value in Zambia
Present innovative ideas for agricultural improvement

sub_topics.short_notes: Agriculture is fundamental to Zambia's economy, providing food security, employment, raw materials for industries, income generation, and foreign exchange earnings. It is both an applied science (using scientific principles) and a technology (practical application of knowledge). Understanding agriculture's importance is crucial for national development.

vocabulary.definition: The use of scientific knowledge for practical purposes, especially in agriculture to improve production
The state of having reliable access to sufficient quantities of affordable, nutritious food
Currency earned through exports of agricultural products to other countries

questions: Explain how agriculture contributes to Zambia's economy
Distinguish between agriculture as an applied science and as a technology
Evaluate the impact of underdeveloped agriculture on Zambia
Propose three innovative ways to improve agriculture in Zambia

sub_topics.title: Agricultural Activities in Zambia
competences: Explore the main agricultural activities in Zambia

teacher_steps: Investigating the main agricultural activities in Zambia: Crop Production (maize, groundnuts, cassava, soya beans, cabbage, citrus, tomatoes, rape...)
Investigating Animal Production activities (cattle, poultry, aquaculture, apiculture, pig production, sheep and goat production, rabbit...)
learner_steps: Research main crops grown in Zambia
Identify livestock and animal production systems
Visit local farms to observe activities

sub_topics.short_notes: Zambia's main agricultural activities include crop production (maize, groundnuts, cassava, soya beans, vegetables like cabbage, tomatoes, and fruits like citrus) and animal production (cattle, poultry, fish farming, beekeeping, pigs, sheep, goats, and rabbits). These activities provide food, income, and employment.

vocabulary.definition: The cultivation and harvesting of plants for food, fiber, or other uses
The raising and management of livestock for meat, milk, eggs, or other products
The farming of fish and other aquatic organisms
The practice of beekeeping for honey and other bee products

questions: List five main crops grown in Zambia
Describe three types of animal production in Zambia
Explain the importance of diversifying agricultural activities
Compare crop production and animal production activities

sub_topics.title: Factors that Influence Agricultural Development in Zambia
competences: Explore factors that influence agricultural development in Zambia

teacher_steps: Investigating factors that influence agricultural development: Environmental factors, social and economic factors, technological factors
Investigating agencies and organisations that support farmers
Illustrating on the map of Zambia: (a) main agro-ecological zones, (b) areas not suitable for dairy farming and ranching, (c) areas not very suitable for growing maize
learner_steps: Research environmental, social, and economic factors
Analyze suitability of different regions for specific farming

sub_topics.short_notes: Agricultural development in Zambia is influenced by environmental factors (climate, soil, water availability), social and economic factors (markets, infrastructure, capital, labor), and technological factors (machinery, improved seeds, farming techniques). Various agencies support farmers including government ministries, NGOs, and cooperatives.

vocabulary.definition: A geographical area with similar climate, soil, and environmental conditions suitable for specific agricultural activities
Basic facilities and systems needed for agriculture, such as roads, markets, and storage facilities
Tools, equipment, and modern techniques that improve agricultural productivity

questions: Identify and explain three factors that influence agricultural development
Name five organizations that support farmers in Zambia
Draw and label the main agro-ecological zones of Zambia
Explain why some areas are not suitable for dairy farming

sub_topics.title: Types of Farmers

teacher_steps: Categorising farmers based on level of production, mechanisation and capital investment
Distinguishing between subsistence farmers, small scale farmers, and commercial farmers
learner_steps: Classify farmers based on production level
Analyze characteristics of each farmer type

sub_topics.short_notes: Farmers are classified based on production level, mechanization, and capital investment: Subsistence farmers (grow for family consumption with minimal surplus), Small-scale farmers (commercial production on limited land with moderate inputs), and Commercial farmers (large-scale production with high mechanization and capital investment).

vocabulary.definition: Farming mainly for family consumption with little or no surplus for sale
Farming on limited land area with moderate inputs and some surplus for market
Large-scale farming primarily for profit with high mechanization and capital investment
The use of machines and equipment in farming operations

questions: Define the three main types of farmers
Compare subsistence and commercial farming
Explain the role of mechanization in classifying farmers
Give examples of each type of farmer from your local area

sub_topics.title: Composition of Soil

teacher_steps: Describing soil composition
Identifying types of rocks based on their physical characteristics (igneous, sedimentary and metamorphic)
Studying soil and its formation (Weathering - physical, chemical and biological)
Analysing soil composition by means of experiments (air, water, mineral particles, organic matter and living organisms)
Analysing the importance of air, water, mineral particles, organic matter and living organisms in the soil
learner_steps: Conduct experiments to examine soil components
Identify rock types in local environment
Analyze experimental results on soil composition
Present findings on importance of each component

sub_topics.short_notes: Soil is composed of five main components: mineral particles (from weathered rocks), water, air, organic matter (humus from decomposed plant and animal material), and living organisms (bacteria, fungi, earthworms). Soil forms through weathering of rocks - physical (breaking down), chemical (changing composition), and biological (organic action). Each component is essential for soil fertility and plant growth.

vocabulary.definition: The breaking down of rocks into smaller particles through physical, chemical, or biological processes
Rock formed from cooled and solidified magma or lava
Rock formed from compressed layers of sediment
Rock formed from existing rocks changed by heat and pressure
Decomposed plant and animal material in soil, also called humus
Small pieces of weathered rock in soil (sand, silt, and clay)

questions: List and explain the five components of soil
Describe the three types of weathering
Conduct an experiment to separate soil components and report findings
Explain the importance of each soil component for plant growth
Identify and classify three types of rocks found in your area

sub_topics.title: Properties of Soil

teacher_steps: Investigating the types of soils (sand, clay and silt)
Determining the physical characteristics of each type of soil (texture, structure and colour)
Determining the chemical characteristics of each type of soil (soil reaction: testing soil pH, and carry out appropriate action)
Profiling soils in the local environment to determine the suitability of the sites for agricultural purposes
learner_steps: Collect soil samples from different locations
Conduct experiments to determine soil texture
Create soil profiles of local sites

sub_topics.short_notes: Soil properties include physical properties (texture - proportion of sand, silt, and clay; structure - arrangement of particles; color - indicating organic matter and minerals) and chemical properties (pH - soil acidity or alkalinity). Soil profiling reveals layers (horizons) and helps determine agricultural suitability. Different soil types have different water-holding capacity, drainage, and nutrient availability.

vocabulary.definition: The relative proportion of sand, silt, and clay particles in soil
The arrangement and organization of soil particles into aggregates
A measure of soil acidity or alkalinity on a scale of 0-14, with 7 being neutral
The largest soil particles, providing good drainage but low water and nutrient retention
Medium-sized soil particles, smooth to touch, moderate water retention
The smallest soil particles, sticky when wet, high water and nutrient retention
A vertical section of soil showing different layers or horizons

questions: Distinguish between sand, silt, and clay soils
Demonstrate how to test soil pH and interpret results
Create a soil profile diagram of a local site
Explain how soil texture affects water retention
Determine the suitability of different soil types for specific crops

sub_topics.title: Soil Fertility

teacher_steps: Describing soil fertility
Investigating factors that determine soil fertility (pH, aeration, drainage, temperature, nutrients, humus, water holding capacity, soil microorganisms, pests and diseases)
Classifying nutrients into major (N, P, K) and minor (boron, zinc, manganese, copper...)
Distinguishing organic fertilisers from inorganic fertilisers
Categorising inorganic fertilisers based on composition (straight and compound fertilisers)
Maintaining soil fertility (crop rotation, mulching, minimum tillage, cover crops...)
Improving soil fertility (liming, organic and inorganic fertiliser application, improved fallow...)
Making compost manure
Investigating soil erosion and its mitigation to maintain soil fertility

sub_topics.short_notes: Soil fertility is the ability of soil to provide nutrients for plant growth. Major factors include pH, nutrients (major: NPK; minor: boron, zinc, manganese, copper), organic matter, aeration, drainage, and soil organisms. Soil fertility is maintained through crop rotation, mulching, minimum tillage, and cover crops. It is improved through liming, fertilizer application (organic: compost, manure; inorganic: straight or compound fertilizers), and improved fallow. Soil erosion reduces fertility and must be prevented.

vocabulary.definition: The ability of soil to provide essential nutrients in adequate amounts for plant growth
Nitrogen (N), Phosphorus (P), and Potassium (K) - nutrients needed in large quantities by plants
Micronutrients like boron, zinc, manganese, copper needed in small quantities
Fertilizer from plant or animal sources, such as compost or manure
Manufactured chemical fertilizer containing specific nutrients
Fertilizer containing only one nutrient (e.g., urea for nitrogen)
Fertilizer containing two or more nutrients (e.g., NPK)
Growing different crops in sequence on the same land to maintain soil fertility
Covering soil with organic material to conserve moisture and add nutrients
Adding lime to acid soil to raise pH and improve fertility
The removal of topsoil by wind or water, reducing soil fertility

questions: Explain factors that determine soil fertility
Distinguish between major and minor nutrients
Compare organic and inorganic fertilizers
Demonstrate how to make compost manure
Describe methods of maintaining and improving soil fertility
Explain how soil erosion affects fertility and propose mitigation measures

sub_topics.title: Soil Water

teacher_steps: Describing soil water and retention of water
Experimenting on drainage and water holding capacity of different soil types
Demonstrating capillarity of different soil types
Analysing the types of soil moisture and their significance to crop production (hygroscopic, gravitational, capillary water...)
Practising the different methods of irrigation (surface, sub surface, overhead irrigation) and their effects on the environment
Interpreting the significance of the water cycle to agriculture
learner_steps: Study and illustrate the water cycle

sub_topics.short_notes: Soil water exists in three forms: gravitational water (drains away by gravity), capillary water (held in small pores, available to plants), and hygroscopic water (tightly bound to particles, unavailable to plants). Different soil types have different water-holding capacities - clay holds most water, sand drains quickly. Irrigation methods include surface (flooding), subsurface (underground pipes), and overhead (sprinklers). The water cycle (evaporation, condensation, precipitation) is essential for agriculture.

vocabulary.definition: The ability of soil to hold water against the force of gravity
Water that drains through soil due to gravity, unavailable to plants
Water held in small pores between soil particles, available to plant roots
Water tightly bound to soil particles, unavailable to plants
The upward movement of water through small pores in soil
Water application by flooding or furrows on the soil surface
Water application through underground pipes or drip systems
Water application from above using sprinklers
The continuous movement of water through evaporation, condensation, and precipitation

questions: Describe the three types of soil moisture and their availability to plants
Compare water-holding capacity of sand, silt, and clay
Demonstrate capillary action in different soil types
Explain three methods of irrigation and their environmental effects
Illustrate and explain the water cycle's significance to agriculture

sub_topics.title: Climate-Smart Agriculture

teacher_steps: Describing Climate-Smart Agriculture
Investigating the key characteristics of Climate-Smart Agriculture
Identifying factors that affect practicing of Climate-Smart Agriculture in Zambia
Analysing the benefits of Climate-Smart Agriculture in Zambia

sub_topics.short_notes: Climate-Smart Agriculture (CSA) is an approach that helps increase agricultural productivity sustainably, adapts and builds resilience to climate change, and reduces greenhouse gas emissions. Key characteristics include sustainable intensification, climate resilience, and mitigation of climate change impacts. Factors affecting CSA in Zambia include access to information, technology, capital, and government support.

vocabulary.definition: Farming practices that increase productivity, enhance resilience to climate change, and reduce greenhouse gas emissions
Increasing agricultural productivity while maintaining environmental sustainability
The ability of agricultural systems to withstand and recover from climate-related shocks
Gases that trap heat in the atmosphere, contributing to climate change

questions: Define Climate-Smart Agriculture
Explain three key characteristics of CSA
Identify factors affecting CSA adoption in Zambia
Analyze the benefits of CSA for Zambian farmers

sub_topics.title: Application of Drones
competences: Use drones to enhance productivity in agriculture

teacher_steps: Describing drones
Identifying types of drones used in agriculture
Evaluating the importance of drones in agriculture
Applying drones in various agricultural purposes

sub_topics.short_notes: Drones (Unmanned Aerial Vehicles) are aircraft operated without a human pilot on board, controlled remotely or autonomously. In agriculture, drones are used for crop monitoring, spraying pesticides, mapping fields, assessing crop health, irrigation management, and livestock monitoring. They improve efficiency, reduce labor costs, and provide precise data for decision-making.

vocabulary.definition: An unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) used for various agricultural applications
Using technology to observe and assess crop health and growth
Using technology and data to optimize agricultural practices and inputs

questions: Define drones and their role in agriculture
Identify three types of agricultural drones
Explain five uses of drones in agriculture
Evaluate the advantages and challenges of using drones in farming

sub_topics.title: Climate Change
competences: Show understanding of climate change and its causes

teacher_steps: Describing climate change
Distinguishing climate change from climate variability
Analysing the effects of greenhouse gas on the climate
Investigating the main causes of climate change (Volcanic activities, wild fires, burning of fossil fuels, farming...)
Relating the effects of climate change to the development of agriculture in Zambia
Identifying ways of mitigating the effects of climate change on agriculture

sub_topics.short_notes: Climate change is long-term alteration in global or regional climate patterns, primarily caused by increased greenhouse gases from human activities. Climate variability refers to short-term fluctuations. Main causes include volcanic activities, wildfires, burning fossil fuels, and agricultural practices. Effects on Zambian agriculture include altered rainfall patterns, droughts, floods, and reduced crop yields. Mitigation includes conservation farming, afforestation, and renewable energy use.

vocabulary.definition: Long-term changes in temperature and weather patterns, mainly caused by human activities
Short-term fluctuations in weather patterns within normal ranges
The trapping of heat in the atmosphere by greenhouse gases
Coal, oil, and natural gas formed from ancient organisms, releasing CO2 when burned
Actions to reduce or prevent greenhouse gas emissions and climate change impacts

questions: Define climate change and distinguish it from climate variability
Explain how greenhouse gases affect climate
Identify and describe four main causes of climate change
Analyze the effects of climate change on Zambian agriculture
Propose five ways to mitigate climate change effects on agriculture
